Investor Anatomy
US Inflation and Price Dynamics
Home
US Inflation and Price Dynamics
Systematically synthesises realised inflation, cost pressures, and market expectations to assess the prevailing US inflation regime.

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime Label: Transitional / Fragile Balance.
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence.
  • The regime is fragile due to deteriorating stability in headline inflation momentum.

Why This Regime:

  • The headline inflation signal has entered a transition band, moving toward a disinflationary regime (1).
  • High-weight indicators for policy inflation and market-priced expectations remain strictly Neutral (2)(3).
  • Dynamic weighting prioritized high-confidence Neutral signals from PCE and term structure over conflicted disinflationary data (2)(4).
  • Wage pressure signals were downweighted due to low interpretation confidence and lagged quarterly inputs (5).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Breakeven rates and the term structure align, showing well-anchored long-term inflation compensation (3)(4).
  • A primary tension exists between the COOL regime label in the inflation signal and its current transition-zone z-score (1).
  • Producer and consumer price divergence remains within historical norms, contradicting the disinflationary impulse in headline data (6).
  • Tensions do not overturn the regime call as most cyclical signals maintain stable neutral classifications.

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant Scenario: Persistent transition toward disinflation as headline momentum compresses toward zero (1).
  • Primary Upside Risk: Sudden reversal in energy or supply chain costs triggering a momentum spike (1).
  • Primary Downside Risk: Cyclical demand collapse forcing a confirmed shift into a deep COOL regime (1).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A 3-month annualized CPI reading exceeding the YoY rate would invalidate the disinflationary bias (1).
  • PCE composite scores moving above 0.75 or below -0.75 would force a regime exit from Neutrality (2).
  • A divergence z-score exceeding 1.0 would signal a transition to a high-pressure cost-push regime (6).
Inputs
Inflation Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Given an inflation_signal z-score of 0.0007 and a regime classification of COOL, this setup aligns with Transition-biased price paths and Indeterminate volatility, where the dominant risk is Regime shift, not trend continuation (1).
  • The signal reflects a state of disinflationary impulse, currently transitioning from Neutral into a COOL regime (1).
  • Conviction Band: Low; Interpretation Confidence: Mixed Signals; Internal Conflict Flag: Yes. Signal Stability Assessment: Deteriorating; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • A composite z-score between -0.25 and +0.25 defines a TRANSITION state, signaling a structural turning point (1).
  • The COOL regime is defined by a z-score ≤ -0.50, though the current data labels values near zero as COOL (1).
  • Economic momentum is assessed by comparing 3-month annualized CPI against the YoY CPI rate (1).
  • Inflation Signal (latest observation: 2026-03-31) (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the rapid compression of the inflation_signal z-score toward zero, falling from 0.347 to 0.0007 in one month (1).
  • Falling values imply a disinflationary slowdown or potential for expansionary policy pivot (1).
  • Internal Conflict: The current z-score sits in the "TRANSITION" band (-0.25 to +0.25), yet the provided regime label identifies it as COOL (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: A 3-month annualized CPI exceeding YoY CPI would signal accelerating momentum and invalidate the disinflationary bias (1).
  • The signal shows deteriorating stability as it approaches a regime-flip threshold.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Persistent transition state leading to a confirmed disinflationary regime.
  • Upside risk: Sharp reversal in 3-month momentum triggered by unexpected supply-chain or energy shocks.
  • Downside risk: Acceleration into a deep COOL regime triggered by a cyclical demand collapse.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months) as the signal tracks 10-year rolling trends and monthly momentum (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium; the transition-zone position necessitates confirmation from liquidity or copper signals (1).

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs aggregate inflation pressure, influencing policy expectations and macro risk budgeting (1).
  • Economic mechanism: Disinflationary impulse or demand deceleration, which typically serves as a tailwind for fixed income (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with M2 liquidity and copper prices; divergence from these would suggest cost-push rather than demand-pull dynamics (1).

Regime Context:

  • Newly entered COOL regime (per provided label), following a period of Neutral stability (1).
  • Direction of change: Strengthening disinflationary momentum.

Model Limitations:

  • Subject to CPI revisions, seasonality, and news-based noise from Economic Policy Uncertainty (EPU) (1).
  • TIPS liquidity bias during stress may distort market-implied components (1).

Data & References:

  • US Inflation Signal (2026-03-31) (1).
  • Influential data: Latest z-score of 0.000778 (1).
  • Additional data: M2 Money Supply and Copper Prices would improve interpretation confidence (1).
Wage Growth and Inflation Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a Wage_Pressure_z of -0.53 and Labor_Tightness_z of -0.17 (1), this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift.
  • The signal is currently in a NEUTRAL regime, indicating wage growth aligned with historical norms (1).
  • Conviction Band: Low; Interpretation Confidence: Low Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • A z-score between -1.0 and +1.0 designates a NEUTRAL regime, implying a stable inflation outlook (1).
  • The Inflation Composite blends Wage Pressure and Labor Market Tightness (LMT) to predict core inflation persistence (1).
  • Weights are applied to Employment Cost Index (ECI) for private and civilian workers to compute pressure (1).
  • Wage Growth and Inflation Signal: observation date not provided.

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the convergence of Private and Civilian Wage YoY growth at 3.3% (1).
  • Stabilisation is evident as the Inflation Composite z-score has remained in the NEUTRAL band for fifteen consecutive observations (1).
  • Internal coherence is high as both wage and labor tightness metrics support a non-inflationary expansion.
  • Conditional Invalidation: An Inflation Composite z-score exceeding +1.0 (HOT regime) (1).
  • The signal exhibits high persistence, having maintained the NEUTRAL classification throughout the provided history.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued range-bound inflation as wage growth tracks the 24-month baseline (1).
  • Upside risk: A sudden spike in Labor_Tightness_z triggering a "HOT" wage-push inflation regime (3).
  • Downside risk: Rising labor market slack leading to a "COOL" disinflationary transition (1).

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months); ECI inputs are quarterly and reflect medium-term cost structures (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Low; data timeliness cannot be assessed from provided content.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the "pricing" macro dimension by measuring the intensity of wage-push inflation (1).
  • Economic mechanism: Inflationary pressure originating from labor costs and supply-demand imbalances (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Typically confirms trends in Policy-Relevant Inflation (PCE) (2) and labor demand signals.

Regime Context:

  • Persistent NEUTRAL regime; no recent transitions observed in the Inflation Composite.
  • Direction of change: Stabilising within the neutral corridor.

Model Limitations:

  • ECI data is quarterly and lagged, requiring interpolation between releases (1).
  • JOLTS-based metrics (used for tightness) are subject to significant survey noise (1).

Data & References:

  • Wage Growth and Inflation Signal, latest observation date not provided (1).
  • Influential datapoints: Wage_Pressure_z (-0.53) and Labor_Tightness_z (-0.17).
  • Additional datasets: US CPI/PCE data (2) and PMI Prices would improve reliability.
Policy-Relevant Inflation (PCE) Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a PCE_Composite of -0.23, headline PCE_YoY of 2.83%, and CorePCE_YoY of 3.06%, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift. (1)
  • The signal is currently in a stable Neutral regime, having transitioned from a Hot regime in late 2023. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Far; Revision Sensitivity: Moderate.

Methodology Applied:

  • Composite scores between -0.75 and 0.75 are classified as Neutral, suggesting no immediate policy bias. (1)
  • The "Level_pp > 0" rule identifies that inflation exceeding the 2% target creates structural pressure for restrictive rates. (1)
  • Momentum is used as an early warning for regime transitions toward Hot or Cool states. (1)
  • Policy-Relevant Inflation (PCE) Signal; Latest observation: 2026-01-31. (1)

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the stabilization of both Headline (-0.22 Z-score) and Core (-0.25 Z-score) inflation within historical norms. (1)
  • Inflation remains above the 2% policy target, maintaining a floor under restrictive monetary policy expectations. (1)
  • Momentum has flattened, with the PCE_Composite showing negligible change from the prior month (-0.234 vs -0.226). (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: A PCE_Composite move exceeding 0.75 would trigger a transition back to a Hot regime. (1)
  • The signal has shown high persistence, remaining in the Neutral regime for over 28 consecutive months. (1)

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued Neutral regime persistence as inflation levels oscillate slightly above the 2% target.
  • Upside risk: A re-acceleration in consumer demand or energy shocks pushing the composite toward the 0.75 "Hot" threshold.
  • Downside risk: Rapid labor market softening causing a transition toward the -0.75 "Cool" disinflationary threshold.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), reflecting the typical lag in PCE data and central bank reaction functions.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, as PCE is the Federal Reserve's primary inflation gauge but currently lacks trend momentum.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the pricing of real rates and the Federal Reserve's "higher for longer" policy stance.
  • Implies an economic transmission mechanism where residual labor market tightness sustains core inflation above target. (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with 10Y Breakeven Inflation and Unemployment rates to validate the sustainability of the current inflation path. (1)

Regime Context:

  • Regime persistence is high; the signal has occupied the Neutral zone since September 2023. (1)
  • Direction of change: Stabilising within the Neutral band.

Model Limitations:

  • Subject to material BEA revisions and monthly reporting lags. (1)
  • Z-scores may be distorted by historical structural breaks in inflation volatility. (1)

Data & References:

  • Policy-Relevant Inflation (PCE) Signal (2026-01-31). (1)
  • Most influential: PCE_Composite (-0.234) and CorePCE_YoY (3.06%). (1)
  • Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean PCE and 10Y Breakeven Inflation Rates would improve reliability. (1)
Inflation Term-Structure Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given an Inflation Term Composite of 0.12 and 5-year breakevens (T5YIE) at 2.56, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift.
  • The signal remains in a Neutral regime as the composite value sits well within the +/- 0.75 threshold (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Far; Revision Sensitivity: Low.

Methodology Applied:

  • Values for the Infl_Term_Composite between -0.75 and 0.75 are classified as Neutral, implying market pricing is within normal historical bands (1).
  • Directional shifts toward +0.75 indicate reflationary expansion, while moves toward -0.75 signal disinflationary tightening (1).
  • The methodology uses weighted Z-scores of 5-year/10-year breakevens and 10-year real TIPS yields to identify regime shifts (1).
  • Dataset: FRED Inflation Term Structure series; Latest Observation: 2026-03-31 (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the stabilization of 10-year real yields (DFII10) at 2.08%, which offsets the rise in 5-year inflation expectations (Z_T5: 0.59) (1).
  • Momentum has shifted from a slight negative bias in February (-0.16) to a mild positive bias in March (0.12), indicating stabilization.
  • Conditional Invalidation: A sustained move in the composite score above 0.75 would trigger a Reflationary regime.
  • The signal has shown high persistence in the Neutral regime for over 24 months, suggesting a lack of structural trend in inflation compensation (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued Neutrality as real yields and expectations remain broadly balanced.
  • Upside risk: Reflationary shift triggered by a sharp rise in 5-year breakevens exceeding 2.75%.
  • Downside risk: Disinflationary shift triggered by real yield expansion (DFII10) above 2.5% without matching inflation expectations.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months). The signal identifies shifts in market-priced regimes relative to rolling historical baselines.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium. Neutral signals act as a secondary conditioning variable rather than a primary driver.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the Inflation dimension of the macro environment.
  • The transmission mechanism involves market-implied inflation compensation and real financial conditions; the current Neutrality implies no immediate pressure on real rates.
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with Liquidity and Credit signals; persistent neutrality suggests an absence of market-priced macro stress (1).

Regime Context:

  • This is a persistent regime; the signal has occupied the Neutral zone since April 2023 (1).
  • Direction of change: Stabilizing near the zero-line after a brief period of mild disinflationary bias in late 2024.

Model Limitations:

  • TIPS liquidity distortions during market stress can create false disinflation signals (1).
  • Monthly resampling introduces a recognition lag for tactical shifts.

Data & References:

  • Dataset: FRED Inflation Term Structure; Latest Observation: 2026-03-31 (1).
  • Most influential datapoints: T5YIE (2.56) and DFII10 (2.08) (1).
  • Additional data: University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment would improve depth by contrasting market-based and survey-based expectations.
Market Implied Inflation Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a T5YIE of 2.56, a T10YIE of 2.31, and a composite z-score of 0.41, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift.
  • The signal is currently in a STABLE regime, reflecting inflation expectations that remain range-bound and consistent with long-term norms (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Low.

Methodology Applied:

  • A z-score between -0.5 and 1.0 classifies the regime as STABLE, implying neutral directional conviction (1).
  • The methodology dictates that "FALLING" signals should be discounted if the broader Liquidity Regime is stressed, which is not currently flagged (1).
  • The composite weights levels (5-year and 10-year breakevens) more heavily than short-term annualised momentum (1).
  • Market Implied Inflation Signal: latest observation dated 2026-03-31 (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the 10-Year Breakeven Inflation Rate (T10YIE) at 2.31, which remains well-anchored within the historical distribution (1).
  • Momentum has stabilised following a minor dip in February, with the MIIE_z recovering from 0.16 to 0.41 in March (1).
  • No significant internal tensions; both 5-year and 10-year metrics show synchronous range-bound behaviour.
  • Conditional Invalidation: A move in the MIIE_z above 1.0 would trigger a "RISING" regime classification.
  • Persistence is high; the STABLE regime has been maintained for over 36 consecutive observations.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued range-bound inflation pricing supported by anchored 10-year expectations.
  • Upside risk: Acceleration in inflation compensation triggered by a sustained break of T5YIE above 2.75.
  • Downside risk: Disinflationary flight-to-quality event if TIPS liquidity premiums spike during market stress.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), as breakevens reflect medium-term market-based compensation rather than high-frequency noise.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, providing a necessary neutral anchor for cross-asset inflation thematic models.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the pricing dimension of the macro cycle, specifically market-based inflation compensation.
  • The current stable direction implies that inflation risk premia are neither expanding nor contracting aggressively.
  • Cycle position: Not determined by this methodology.
  • Typically interacts with real yields and realized CPI to identify whether market expectations lead or lag fundamental shifts (1).

Regime Context:

  • This is a persistent regime; the signal has occupied the STABLE band throughout the provided historical window.
  • Direction of change: Stabilising, following a brief period of softening in early Q1 2026.

Model Limitations:

  • TIPS liquidity premiums can artificially depress breakevens during periods of acute financial stress (1).
  • Monetary policy interventions, such as QE, may distort term premia and decouple expectations from fundamentals (1).

Data & References:

  • Market Implied Inflation Signal (MIIE) dataset: latest observation 2026-03-31.
  • The MIIE_score of 65.1 is the most influential factor for the current regime classification.
  • Real interest rate trends and realized PCE data would improve interpretation depth.
CPI vs PPI Divergence Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a divergence_z of 0.23, a CPI_YoY of 2.43%, and a PPI_YoY of 3.22%, this setup aligns with range-biased price paths and normal volatility, where the dominant risk is mean reversion because the abs(z) < 0.35 threshold indicates a Neutral regime, not a trend continuation.
  • The signal is currently classified as NEUTRAL, indicating the producer-to-consumer price gap is within historical noise thresholds (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: High.

Methodology Applied:

  • Divergence thresholds: abs(z) < 0.35 is defined as NEUTRAL, representing a stable price relationship (2).
  • HOT regime requires z >= 1.0 or (z >= 0.75 and CPI YoY >= 3.0%) to signal cost-push pressure (1).
  • The methodology treats producer prices as a leading indicator for consumer price direction (1).
  • CPI vs PPI Divergence Signal: latest observation February 28, 2026 (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the narrowing gap between PPI and CPI, with the PPI_minus_CPI_YoY spread currently at 0.78 (1).
  • Recent momentum shows a shift from negative to positive divergence, though it remains below the 1.0 z-score threshold for a regime shift (1).
  • Stabilisation in the NEUTRAL band has persisted for over 28 months (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: Divergence z-score exceeding 1.0 or falling below -1.0.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant; risks balanced due to stable price relationship.
  • Upside risk: PPI acceleration beyond CPI causing margin compression and a transition to HOT.
  • Downside risk: PPI collapse relative to CPI leading to a return to the COOL (disinflation) regime.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months) as it identifies multi-month pipeline inflation trends.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium as the signal currently lacks extreme directional bias but identifies core stability.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the pricing and inflation dimension by measuring relative speeds of production and retail price changes (1).
  • The implied economic mechanism is pipeline price pass-through; current neutrality suggests no significant upstream pressure on retail margins.
  • Cycle position: Not determined by methodology.
  • Typically interacts with inflation breakevens and ISM Prices Paid to confirm cost-push environments (1).

Regime Context:

  • Persistent Neutral regime since October 2023 (1).
  • Direction of change: Stabilising, with a slight strengthening of the producer price component relative to consumer prices.

Model Limitations:

  • PPI is subject to multi-month revisions which can retroactively change z-score classifications (1).
  • Pass-through from producer to consumer prices is often lagged and incomplete (1).

Data & References:

  • Latest dataset observation: 2026-02-28 (1).
  • Dominant datapoints: divergence_z (0.23) and PPI_YoY (3.22) (1).
  • Additional indicators: ISM Prices Paid and Inflation Breakevens would improve reliability.
Investor Anatomy - US Inflation and Price Dynamics - Not Investment Advice
The information presented is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute financial or investment advice. It has been prepared without regard to individual objectives, financial situation, or needs. You should consider whether it is appropriate for your circumstances and seek independent advice where necessary.
